SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims (that a compare-aggregate framework is effective across multiple sequence-matching tasks and that element-wise comparison functions such as SUB/MULT work well) are supported by experiments on four datasets (MovieQA, InsuranceQA, WikiQA, SNLI) and by ablation analyses (Sec. 3 and 3.4). Mathematical definitions and model components (preprocessing, attention, six comparison functions in Sec. 2.2–2.3, aggregation in Sec. 2.4) are well-specified and appear correct. Methodological weaknesses include relying on reported numbers for baselines rather than re-implementing them and the absence of statistical significance testing, which weakens comparative claims. Overall, results presented justify the conclusions but would be stronger with re-implemented baselines and significance analyses.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new framework – it builds on existing 'compare-aggregate' ideas (cites Parikh et al., Wang & Jiang, He & Lin) – but it contributes a systematic cross-dataset evaluation and an explicit comparison of six comparison functions (notably element-wise SUB, MULT and their combination in Sec. 2.3). The techniques are a non-trivial empirical study and consolidation rather than a wholly novel algorithmic invention; several components (attention, CNN aggregation, element-wise ops) are known from prior work. An expert would view this as a meaningful empirical/engineering contribution but largely incremental in method novelty.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized (Intro, Method with clear subsections, Experiments, Related Work, Conclusion) and states contributions early (end of Intro). Notation for matrices Q, A, dimensions and the six comparison functions is consistent and equations are provided (e.g., Eqns. 1–9, Sec. 2). Figures/tables are referenced (e.g., Figure 1, Table 2/3) and experiments are described with sufficient implementation detail (embeddings, optimization). Minor repetitiveness and some high-level choices (order-insensitive preprocessing) could be explained in more depth, but overall the exposition is clear.
